entry.title: Step-by-Step Guide to Use an Attribute Other Than LoginName for Seamless Authentication
entry.content:
Step 1: Access IAS Administration Console 

  1. Log in to the SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) administration console.
  2. Navigate to the "Applications & Resources" section.

Step 2: Open the Application Configuration

  1. Select the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) OpenID application for which you want to swap the LoginName attribute. 
  2.  Ex: SCA_OpenID_XXXX

Step 3: Define New Attribute for LoginName & Modify Attribute Mappings

  1. Go to the "Attribute Mappings" tab, where the current mappings for the user attributes are listed.
  2. Find the mapping for login_name  and Identify the new mapping attribute that you want to use in place of  login_name. For example, it could be customAttribute1, Email, or any other valid attribute in your user store which is used to UNIQUELY identify the USER in USER STORE.(usually mapped to LoginName by default).
  3.  

     

  4. Change the mapping for login_name:

    • Replace the existing attribute (e.g., LoginName) with the new attribute (e.g., Email).
  5. Save the changes  

Step 4: SAML changes on Incentive Management 

  1. Go to Commissions Application.
  2. Click on Subject Name Identifier and set to the New Attribute i.e. EMAIL

  3.  

  4. Login into Commissions and try to launch Embedded Analytics application
  5. User should be able to launch SAC without any issues.

Step 5: Other Dependent EA Applications

  1. Similarly, set the mapping to new Attribute "Email" for other EA applications under 'Assertion Attributes' to login_Name
  2. Ex: EA_STORAGE_SERVICE_XXXX , SCAI_OPENID_XXXX ,EA_ADMIN_XXXX except SalesCloud SAML Application Sales Cloud Analytics - XXXX
  3. NOTE:  that Sales Cloud Analytics - XXXX is used for SAML as SAC authenticates user with P-number there will be NO changes are recommended.
  4.  EA_STORAGE_SERVICE_XXXX
  5.  

     

  6. SCAI_OPENID_XXXX
  7.  

     

  8. EA_ADMIN_XXXX

  9.  

Step 6:  Target Transformation Changes Required from SAC side to map to new Attribute.

  1. Go to IPS Target Systems.
  2. Click on SAC_TARGET_SYSTEM_XXXX
  3. Click on Edit link under Transformations Section
  4. Here in this case the new mapping attribute is 'Email', hence 'userName' should be replaced to 'Email'. 

          {
                "sourcePath": "$.userName",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:user-custom-parameters:1.0']['extensionProperty1']"
            },

       replace with 

         {
                "sourcePath": "$.emails[0].value",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:user-custom-parameters:1.0']['extensionProperty1']"
            },

#SLIThoughtLeadership

entry.title: Where can I start learning planning techniques using SAP S/4HANA?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Learning journey Exploring Business Processes in SAP S/4HANA Production Planning learning journey.

Overview
The course "Business Processes in SAP S/4HANA Production Planning" is designed to teach advanced planning techniques using SAP S/4HANA. It includes understanding the basics and detailed steps of supply chain planning, various demand management strategies like make-to-order and make-to-stock, predictive material requirement planning (pMRP), material requirements planning, capacity evaluation, techniques for demand-driven replenishment, and advanced multistep planning scenarios. The course targets roles such as project managers, application consultants, and business process architects, offering practical exercises and comprehensive insights into production planning using SAP S/4HANA.

Learning objectives

  • Understand the basics and detailed steps of supply chain planning.
  • Apply various demand management strategies, including make-to-order and make-to-stock.
  • Implement predictive material requirement planning (pMRP).
  • Execute material requirements planning and capacity evaluation.
  • Optimize supply chain efficiency using demand-driven replenishment techniques.
  • Navigate through and manage advanced multistep planning scenarios.

Prerequisites

  • Discovering the Basics of SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area. 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Configuring VAT with Prompt Payment Discount in the UK
entry.content:
Understanding VAT Adjustments and Prompt Payment Discounts in the UK

From April 1st, 2015, a significant change to the UK VAT legislation regarding Prompt Payment Discount (PPD) came into effect. This change impacts how suppliers and customers manage VAT (Value Added Tax) on transactions involving prompt payment discounts.

The details of this legal requirement can be found at the following URL:  VAT guide (VAT Notice 700) - GOV.UK section 7.3.2

In the United Kingdom, managing VAT (Value Added Tax) accurately is crucial for both suppliers and customers. Suppliers must account for VAT on received payments, while customers need to recover the VAT paid. HMRC (Her Majesty's Revenue and Customs) provides two options to ensure proper VAT accounting:

  1. Issue a Credit Note: This evidences the reduction in consideration.
  2. Include Specific Terms on the Invoice: This includes the terms of the Prompt Payment Discount and a statement that the customer can only recover the VAT paid to the supplier as input tax.

Ensuring Compliance with HMRC Rulings

To comply with HMRC rulings and manage VAT adjustments and prompt payment discounts without issuing a credit memo, it is recommended to use the gross/gross configuration. This involves unchecking the net/net settings in your company code settings.

Important Note:

If your system is currently configured with net/net settings and contains open accounts payable or accounts receivable, ensure these open items are cleared with your current settings before making any changes.

Authorizations Required

- Configuration Expert - Business Process Configuration (SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT): This role is necessary to configure Edit Tax Information for Company Codes and Define Countries/Regions configuration activities.
- General Ledger (SAP_BR_GL_ACCOUNTANT): This role is required to configure the Manage G/L Account Master Data application.

Configuration Procedure

1. Edit Tax Information for Company Codes (ID:105675):
- Ensure that the fields "Tax base is net value" and "Discount base is net value" are unticked.

2. Define Countries/Regions (ID:105675):
- Ensure that "Net Discount Base" and "Net Tax Base" are unticked.

3. Manage G/L Account Master Data Application:
- Ensure the Tax Category is set to "All tax types allowed." for your discount General ledgers accounts. The tax category setting is critical to ensure VAT is adjusted on payment if the Prompt Payment Discount is taken.

- Ensure the field "Postings with tax allowed" is ticked.

Note:

The settings in point 2 are required if RITA (Real-Time Information and Tax Adjustments) is activated. 

Examples of Prompt Payment Discount in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the end-to-end customer discount process is a component of accounts receivable management. It allows suppliers to offer customers a discount for settling invoices within a specific time frame. For this to be effective:

1. The supplier must offer the discount through an appropriate payment term in a sales or service order.
2. The customer must claim the discount by paying within the specified time frame.

Let’s now look at the process in the system, which starts with the sales representative of the supplying company choosing the desired payment terms in a Sales or Service order.

By following these guidelines, businesses can ensure accurate VAT accounting and compliance with HMRC regulations, while also benefiting from prompt payment discounts.

Example 1:

Step 1: Sales Representative Chooses Payment Terms

The journey begins with the sales representative of the supplying company. When creating a Sales or Service order, they select the desired payment terms. For instance, a common term might be offering a 2% discount on the invoice gross amount if the customer pays within a specified period.

Step 2: Customer Activates the Discount

Assume the customer decides to take advantage of this 2% discount. If the original invoice amount is £720, the customer would pay £705.60 by adhering to the payment terms.

Step 3: Accounting Records the Payment

Now, we switch perspectives to the accounting business user. When the customer makes the payment, the accounting clerk records this incoming payment. The system requires specific input parameters, such as the posting date and the amount paid.

Step 4: System Determines the Discount

Based on the provided information, the system automatically identifies that the customer has activated the 2% discount by paying early. It calculates the difference between the paid amount (£705.60) and the original invoice amount (£720), recognizing the £14.40 as the discount.

Step 5: Journal Entry Postings

Finally, the system makes the necessary journal entry postings. It books the revenue reduction due to the cash discount into the expense account, ensuring accurate financial records.

Example Accounts Receivable: Gross/Gross and Net/Net Settings:

When managing accounts receivable, it's essential to understand the differences between gross/gross and net/net settings, especially when dealing with VAT (Value Added Tax). Let's break down these concepts with examples.

Outgoing Invoice with Gross/Gross Settings:

Incoming Payment: 

- Payment: £705.60
- Input Tax (20%):** £117.60

In this scenario, the input tax of £117.60 is calculated as 20% of the gross amount. This matches the accounts payable of £120, adjusted by £2.40 (VAT adjustment), resulting in £117.60.

Outgoing Invoice Net/Net Settings:

Incoming Payment: 

The payment, if paid within PPD terms in accounts receivable is £705,6 including VAT £117.60. This represents 20 % VAT where the gross value is lowered by discount offered, that is £600 - £12 = £588).  The calculation here would be ok. 

 However, if paid after 14 days payment is £717.60 but the VAT is calculated at £117.60.  In this scenario this does not equate to 20%..

Options to Address Discrepancies:

1. Lower the Gross Amount:
- Adjust the gross amount from £600 to £588 and calculate VAT at £117.60 to maintain the 20% rate.

2. Issue Credit Notes:
- If the PPD is taken, issue credit notes to correct the VAT calculation.

Understanding these settings and their implications ensures accurate financial reporting and compliance with VAT regulations.

If you have any questions or need further clarification, feel free to reach out!
